_meta.description: Geopolitical lens. heatmap: chokepoint scored 1-5 on [supply concentration, chokepoint severity, Western policy response, AI-race relevance]. actor_map: who controls each broad stage by country (Design/IP, Equipment, Fabrication, Packaging/System).

heatmap.det: ASML (Netherlands) is the sole EUV maker; Zeiss optics + Cymer source are single-source within it. The hardest-to-replicate node in the supply chain. NL+US+Japan jointly bar advanced tools to China — a binding policy lever. China's SMEE is ~a decade behind. There is no leading-edge AI silicon without ASML.

TSMC has ~90%+ of <5nm logic, overwhelmingly in Taiwan — the single largest geographic concentration risk in the AI era. Arizona/Japan/Germany fabs diversify at the margin but the centre of gravity stays in Taiwan. A Taiwan disruption would halt the global AI buildout; this is the defining tail risk.

HBM gates AI GPU shipments and is ~95% Korean (SK Hynix + Samsung) plus Micron (US). SK Hynix ~62% share and Nvidia's lead supplier. All sold out for 2026. Less politicized than logic, but a Korea-centric single point of dependency for every accelerator. China (CXMT) years behind.

TSMC CoWoS is the gating bottleneck on AI GPU output; concentrated in Taiwan with ABF substrates from Japan (Ibiden/Shinko) and Taiwan (Unimicron). Amkor's Arizona fab is the main Western reshoring response. Capacity, not technology, is the constraint here — and it compounds the Taiwan concentration.

Synopsys + Cadence (~70%+) + Arm gate all advanced design — a US/UK chokepoint and a fast on/off lever (BIS halted China EDA sales May 2025, reversed July 2025 in a rare-earth swap). China's Empyrean is far behind on full-flow. The clearest example of Western leverage in the stack.

Japan controls ~80-90% of advanced photoresist (JSR, TOK, Shin-Etsu, Fujifilm) and ~50%+ of 300mm wafers (Shin-Etsu, SUMCO). Quiet, under-discussed upstream chokepoints sitting above even TSMC. Japan demonstrated willingness to use materials leverage (vs Korea, 2019).

EML lasers are made by <5 firms (Lumentum, Coherent, Mitsubishi, Sumitomo, Broadcom) — a deep chokepoint Nvidia spent ~$4B to lock. China dominates transceiver module assembly (~63%; InnoLight+Eoptolink ~60% of Nvidia 800G) but US silicon (DSPs) + lasers capture the value. A split US-value / China-assembly structure.

Harmonic/RV reducers (Harmonic Drive, Nabtesco ~60%), roller screws (NSK/THK/GSA) and torque motors are historically Japanese/European chokepoints. China (Inovance, Green Harmonic, Sanhua) is aggressively localizing to cut humanoid cost — Optimus Gen-3 reportedly ~70% China-sourced. The contested frontier of the next hardware wave.

The make-or-break AR components: waveguides (Lumus/Israel, Dispelix, DigiLens), high-index glass (Schott, Corning, Ohara) and micro-displays (Sony OLEDoS, JBD microLED). Pre-mass-market so not yet politicized, but China (Goertek) already dominates device assembly and is moving up into displays (BOE, JBD).

China's parallel stack — SMIC 7nm + Huawei Ascend + CXMT HBM — is the central US-China tech-war front and the most heavily policy-targeted node (Entity List, EDA/WFE/HBM curbs). Fenced off from TSMC + Nvidia + EUV; progressing on DUV multipatterning at high cost. The self-sufficiency drive is a national priority.

The binding physical constraint on the AI buildout is now power and cooling, not chips. Grid access, multi-year transformer lead times and the jump to liquid cooling (~76% of mix by 2026) gate deployment. Less a country chokepoint than an infrastructure one — but it's where AI hardware meets the energy supply chain (cross-link to the Hard Assets map).

l.t: ASIC design svcs Alchip, GUC, MediaTek
JCET OSAT; InnoLight optics; Goertek/Inovance assembly
ASML (NL) EUV; Zeiss, Trumpf (DE); BESI (NL); VAT (CH)
